name: Guan Yin
description: Guan Yin, also known as Avalokiteśvara in Sanskrit, is a bodhisattva revered in Mahayana Buddhism, particularly in East Asia. Often referred to as the Goddess of Mercy or Compassion, Guan Yin embodies the compassionate nature of all Buddhas. Guan Yin is an important figure in Chinese folk religion and is worshiped in many temples and homes. Unlike some deities rooted in specific historical figures, Guan Yin's origins are traced to the bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara, whose attributes of compassion and mercy were gradually feminized and localized as Buddhism spread into China. The bodhisattva's devotion to alleviating suffering has made Guan Yin a popular deity, especially among women seeking children or protection. Depictions of Guan Yin vary, but common representations include a serene figure holding a vase containing pure water or a willow branch, symbolizing purification and healing. The belief in Guan Yin's ability to hear the cries of the world and offer immediate assistance has cemented Guan Yin's position as one of the most beloved and widely venerated deities in Chinese culture.
subtitle: Goddess of Mercy

keyMyths.title: The Thousand Arms
keyMyths.description: One popular myth recounts Guan Yin vowing to postpone her own Buddhahood until all sentient beings were liberated from suffering. Overwhelmed by the magnitude of the task, her head split into eleven pieces, and her arms shattered. Amitabha Buddha, witnessing her plight, transformed the fragments into eleven heads and a thousand arms, empowering her to reach out and help countless beings simultaneously. This myth illustrates Guan Yin’s boundless compassion and unwavering commitment to alleviating suffering.
keyMyths.source: Blofeld, J. (1979). Bodhisattva of Compassion: The Mystical Cult of Kuan Yin.

keyMyths.title: Guan Yin and Miao Shan
keyMyths.description: The legend of Miao Shan is a prominent narrative associated with Guan Yin. Miao Shan was a princess who refused to marry, choosing instead to become a Buddhist nun. Her father, angered by her decision, subjected her to harsh treatment. Eventually, she was executed. However, she descended into Hell and liberated the suffering souls there. Upon returning to the world, she miraculously healed her father, demonstrating the power of compassion and filial piety. This story is often used to explain Guan Yin's association with healing and salvation.
keyMyths.source: Yü, C. F. (1976). The Chinese Transformation of Avalokiteśvara.

keyMyths.title: Saving from the Burning Building
keyMyths.description: Many stories circulate of Guan Yin appearing to people in times of dire need. One common tale involves a person trapped in a burning building. By sincerely reciting Guan Yin's name, the person is miraculously saved from the flames. This myth reinforces the belief in Guan Yin's immediate availability to offer aid and protection to those who call upon her with faith. It highlights her role as a savior in moments of crisis and underscores the importance of devotion.
keyMyths.source: Martin, P. (2017). Guan Yin: The Chinese Transformation of Avalokitesvara.

sources.author: John Blofeld

sources.author: Chün-fang Yü
sources.work: Kuan-yin: The Chinese Transformation of Avalokiteśvara
sources.description: Explores the evolution of Avalokiteśvara into the Chinese deity Guan Yin, focusing on the cultural and religious factors that shaped this transformation.

sources.author: Paul Martin
sources.description: Examines the diverse iconography of Guan Yin and its implications for understanding the deity's multifaceted nature.
